steps.title: Identify the appositive relationship
steps.content: The blank separates "French portrait artist" from "Marie-Denise Villers (1774–1821)." The second noun phrase is an appositive—it renames or identifies the artist.

steps.title: Apply the restrictive appositive rule
steps.content:
Rule: a restrictive appositive — one that identifies exactly which person is meant — takes no punctuation. "Marie-Denise Villers (1774-1821)" tells us which little-known French portrait artist; it is essential, not decorative.

A: "...portrait artist— Marie-Denise Villers..." — an em dash introduces elaboration or a nonrestrictive aside. The name here is an identifier, not an aside. Incorrect.

B: "...portrait artist Marie-Denise Villers..." — no punctuation; the name directly and necessarily identifies the artist. Correct.

C: "...portrait artist: Marie-Denise Villers..." — a colon introduces a list or explanation after an independent clause; the name is a simple identifier, not a listed elaboration. Incorrect.

D: "...portrait artist, Marie-Denise Villers..." — a comma signals a nonrestrictive appositive (optional extra info), implying the name could be removed. It cannot. Incorrect.
